design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore dungeons/wilderness, defeat enemies, collect items and experience, gather staff pieces from faction-controlled areas, defeat final boss Hecubah
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Class identity lock-in creates divergent playstyles; player must navigate world designed for three different character fantasies simultaneously (warrior vs conjurer vs wizard)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Be transported to a high fantasy world and become a powerful hero capable of returning home by mastering either martial prowess, summoning magic, or arcane spellcraft
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Radical class differentiation—gameplay fundamentally changes based on initial choice (warrior uses no magic/bows, conjurer summons and tames, wizard maximizes spells but sacrifices armor/HP). Three completely separate linear storylines reward class identity. Magic system inspired by Magic: The Gathering with spell combinations and traps. Fog of war 'TrueSight' system dynamically reveals only line-of-sight areas—opinionated about exploration pacing and information denial.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Class selection at game start determines core difficulty profile; wizard has low HP but highest spell versatility; warrior trades magical flexibility for durability; conjurer balances summons and ranged damage. Equipment durability system adds resource management tension (weapons/armor wear and require repair or replacement).

progression.upgrade_system: Experience points, item collection (weapons with enchantments, armor with elemental resistances), spell learning via Beast Scrolls for conjurers, equipment repair via NPC traders. Enchanted weapons add magical bonuses to physical damage; armor can protect against fire, poison, etc.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Collect three pieces of the Staff of Oblivion guarded by factions representing unselected classes; assemble staff to defeat Hecubah. Class-specific quest lines unlock unique narrative endings.
replayability_hooks: Three distinct class-based campaigns with unique storylines and endings
Class selection fundamentally alters accessible weapons, spells, and playstyle
Multiplayer modes (deathmatch, capture the flag, Flagball, King of the Realm, Elimination) with clan ladder ranking
NoxQuest cooperative expansion adds replayable co-op campaign

world_building_techniques: Loading screen exposition explaining backstory (Jandor hero, Necromancers trapped in Orb, Staff disassembled among factions)
Pre-rendered full-motion videos at key moments
Environmental storytelling through faction-controlled locations (Fire Knights' Dün Mir, Castle Galava wizards, Temple of Ix conjurers)

boss_design.philosophy: Final confrontation with Hecubah, the evil sorceress pulling Jack into the parallel world, serves as culmination of class-specific narrative arc. Boss design likely emphasizes magical difficulty given sorceress nature and Magic: The Gathering-inspired spell system.

faction_indicators: Fire Knights of Dün Mir (warrior faction, guards Halberd of Horrendous)
Castle Galava wizards (arcane faction, guards Heart of Nox)
Temple of Ix conjurers (summoning faction, guards Weirdling Beast)
Necromancers (ancient defeated faction, souls trapped in Orb)
danger_signals: Hecubah's necromantic army threatening the land
Orb containing trapped Necromancer souls represents existential threat
Parallel dimension transport mechanism suggests dimensional instability
environmental_storytelling: Ogre village (raised the female infant revealed as Hecubah's likely origin)
Jandor's legacy scattered across three faction strongholds
Dungeon and wilderness exploration reveals class-appropriate encounters and loot
NPC allies temporarily accompany player to assist or require rescue
